attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset lower back pain after lifting a heavy box of books. He reports a background of early morning stiffness and back pain that improves with movement over the past year. He has been experiencing increasing fatigue over several months, struggling to complete daily activities. He has lost 5 kg in weight. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears tired but well. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. There is tenderness over the lumbar spine but normal range of movement. Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable.

Laboratory investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 82 g/L (135-175)

  • White cell count: 4.2 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Serum calcium: 2.59 mmol/L (2.1-2.55)

  • Serum sodium: 128 mmol/L (133-145)

  • Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 85 mm/hr (<20)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features that could initially suggest several conditions, but the combination of findings points towards multiple myeloma. Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell malignancy that typically affects older adults and presents with bone pain, anaemia, and hypercalcaemia due to bone marrow infiltration and osteolytic lesions. The hyponatraemia represents pseudohyponatraemia due to hyperproteinaemia from the underlying plasma cell dyscrasia. The elevated ESR and weight loss further support this diagnosis.

Musculoskeletal back pain might explain the acute back pain following lifting, but would not account for the significant anaemia, hypercalcaemia, hyponatraemia, or systemic symptoms of fatigue and weight loss. The elevated ESR would also be unusual for simple musculoskeletal pain.

Primary hyperparathyroidism could explain the hypercalcaemia but would not cause significant anaemia or hyponatraemia. While it can cause fatigue and bone pain, the degree of anaemia and elevated ESR would be unusual.

Adrenal insufficiency typically presents with fatigue, weight loss, and hyponatraemia but would cause hypocalcaemia rather than hypercalcaemia. Cortisol deficiency leads to decreased calcium absorption and increased calcium excretion.

Ankylosing spondylitis could explain the morning stiffness, back pain that improves with movement, and elevated ESR. However, it typically affects younger patients and would not cause such significant anaemia or hypercalcaemia. The age of onset makes this diagnosis less likely.

The combination of findings in this case warrants urgent investigation for multiple myeloma, including serum protein electrophoresis, urinary Bence Jones proteins, and skeletal survey or CT imaging to assess for lytic lesions.
learningPoints: Back pain, hypercalcaemia and anaemia are key features of multiple myeloma that should prompt further investigation.
Pseudohyponatraemia can occur in multiple myeloma due to hyperproteinaemia from plasma cell dyscrasia.
The combination of fatigue, bone pain, anaemia and hypercalcaemia should raise suspicion for malignancy.
references: Tomlinson R. Multiple myeloma. Australian Journal for General Practitioners. 2018;47:526-9.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
